---
title: "Client Report - Famous Names"
subtitle: "Unit 1 Task 3"
author: "Camila Rojas"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
df = pd.read_csv("https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4names/raw/master/data-raw/names_year/names_year.csv")
```



## QUESTION 1

<!-- Make sure at least one of your charts includes reference marks!! -->

__Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul are all Christian names. From 1920 - 2000, compare the name usage of each of the four names in a single chart. What trends do you notice? You must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.__ 

From 1920 to 2000, Mary was used much more than Martha, Peter, and Paul. Mary was very popular in the earlier years but started to decline after about the 1960s. Paul also increased in use around the 1940s and 1950s, then slowly went down. Peter and Martha stayed much less common throughout the whole period. Overall, the chart shows that these Christian names were more popular earlier in the 1900s and became less common over time as naming trends changed.

```{python}
# Q1

# Keep only the names and years we need
four = df.query('name == ["Mary", "Martha", "Peter", "Paul"] and year >= 1920 and year <= 2000')

# Plot how each name changes over time
(
    ggplot(four, aes(x="year", y="Total", color="name"))
    + geom_line()
    + scale_x_continuous(format="d")  # years without commas
    + labs(
        title="Use of Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul (1920–2000)",
        x="Year",
        y="Name Count",
        color="Name"
    )
)

```


## QUESTION 2

1. __Think of a unique name from a famous movie. Plot the usage of that name and see how changes line up with the movie release. Does it look like the movie had an effect on usage? You must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.__  

The name Elsa stays very low for most of the years shown in the chart. After 2013, marked by the dashed line for the release of Frozen, the usage of the name increases a lot. Since this change happens right after the movie came out, it looks like the movie likely influenced more people to choose the name Elsa.

```{python}
# Q2

# I filtered the data to only keep the name Elsa from the movie Frozen 

elsa = df.query('name == "Elsa"')

# Frozen came out in 2013, so I added a vertical dashed line as a reference mark
(
    ggplot(elsa, aes(x="year", y="Total"))
    + geom_line(color="blue")                 
    + geom_vline(xintercept=2013,
    linetype="dashed", color="red")
    + scale_x_continuous(format="d")
    + labs(
        title="Use of the name Elsa over time",
        x="Year",
        y="Name Count"
    )
)

```
